one_line_takeaway: An industry deployment report describing a four-layer defense-in-depth architecture (gVisor sandboxing, credential-proxy sidecar, Kubernetes egress allowlisting, and a prompt-integrity framework) built to harden a real nine-agent OpenClaw fleet processing healthcare PHI, backed by a 90-day hardening timeline and a same-day-remediated fleet audit that found four HIGH-severity credential-exposure findings, but without adversarial re-testing of the deployed defenses.
key_finding: The authors' own fleet security audit (conducted March 11, 2026, on the pre-Kubernetes-migration VM fleet) found four HIGH-severity issues, all remediated same-day: H1 -- 12 credential exports in .bashrc (VM 'Galadriel'); H2 -- openclaw.json world-readable at mode 664 (Galadriel); H3 -- openclaw.json world-readable at mode 644 (VM 'Boromir'); H4 -- AWS Bedrock key exported in .bashrc (VM 'Gandalf'). Four MEDIUM findings (M1-M3 scattered workspace .env files on Gandalf; M4 workspace .env with tokens on Boromir) remained open at time of writing. Six of nine fleet VMs (CEO, Eowyn, Gildor, Strider, Elrond, Legolas) had no findings above LOW. The authors report the credential proxy sidecar (their own L2 design) eliminates this entire class of finding by removing raw secrets from agent containers altogether. Separately, the paper's own coverage-mapping claims direct mitigation of 9 of Shapira et al.'s 11 case studies, partial mitigation of 1 (CS#7, emotional/guilt manipulation -- reduced but not eliminated by anti-manipulation prompt rules), and explicitly scopes 1 (CS#6, provider-level model censorship) as out of scope since it is a model-provider issue rather than a deployment issue. gVisor sandboxing overhead is reported as ~2-5ms added TCP connection latency (negligible against 500-3000ms model inference latency) and ~20-40% higher sequential file I/O overhead, characterized by the authors as not impacting agent response times since file I/O is not on the inference critical path.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (as distinct from the cited Shapira et al. red-teaming study it builds on) is: (1) a six-domain threat model for autonomous AI agents in healthcare (credential exposure, execution capability abuse, network egress exfiltration, prompt integrity failures, database access risks, fleet configuration drift) that maps each domain to specific HIPAA Security Rule provisions; (2) a four-layer defense-in-depth architecture the authors designed and deployed -- (L1) gVisor kernel-level sandboxing on Kubernetes, (L2) a credential proxy sidecar that keeps raw secrets out of agent containers, (L3) Kubernetes NetworkPolicy-enforced per-agent egress allowlists, and (L4) an application-layer 'prompt integrity framework' with cryptographically structured trusted metadata envelopes, untrusted-content labeling, and five anti-injection rules in AGENTS.md; (3) an automated fleet security audit agent ('Tony') built and operated by the authors, with real findings from its production runs; and (4) a 90-day longitudinal account of progressively hardening a real production fleet of nine OpenClaw agents (running Claude via AWS Bedrock on GCP) through three VM-image hardening generations toward the Kubernetes target architecture. This is an industry deployment/architecture report, not a controlled experimental study with held-out baselines.
threat_model: The paper's own six-domain threat model, developed specifically for a production healthcare-tech agent fleet: (1) Credential Exposure -- API keys/tokens for model providers, GitHub, Linear, Slack, Telegram, Grafana, Sentry stored in openclaw.json/.env/.bashrc; (2) Execution Capability Abuse -- shell/filesystem access, package installation, sometimes sudo, enabling lateral movement, backdoors, cross-agent corruption; (3) Network Egress Exfiltration -- unrestricted outbound HTTP/email/messaging enabling PHI exfiltration to attacker-controlled endpoints; (4) Prompt Integrity and Indirect Injection -- instruction/data conflation in the LLM context window, identity spoofing via display-name manipulation, injection via externally editable linked documents; (5) Database Access and PHI Exposure -- agents querying clinical databases without row/column-level restrictions or auditing; (6) Fleet Configuration Drift -- inconsistent hardening/versions across VMs creating exploitable inconsistency. Each domain is explicitly mapped in the paper's Table 1 to a HIPAA Security Rule provision (e.g., 45 CFR 164.312(a) Access Controls, 164.312(b) Audit Controls, 164.312(e) Transmission Security, 164.308(a)(8) Evaluation) and to a validating case study, either from Shapira et al.'s red-teaming (cited work) or the authors' own fleet audit findings.
limitations: The paper explicitly states its own limitations: (1) the prompt integrity layer (L4) is called 'the most brittle' of the four layers because, unlike the three infrastructure layers, it depends on the LLM correctly following instructions about how to interpret inputs rather than an infrastructure boundary the agent cannot manipulate via natural language -- the authors state it 'reduces the attack surface significantly' but 'cannot provide the same level of assurance as the infrastructure layers.' (2) The 'Audit Agent Paradox': the audit agent Tony requires elevated SSH/IAM privileges across the whole fleet to function, making it the highest-value target in the fleet; the authors' mitigations (service-account scoping, immutable audit logging) address direct attack paths but do not eliminate this structural tension, and they flag alternative audit architectures (non-agent scanners, HSM-backed audit trails, split privilege models) as future work rather than solved problems. (3) CS#7 (guilt/emotional-manipulation attacks) is only partially mitigated. (4) The security posture data comes from a single production deployment at one company with a fleet of nine agents -- there is no external replication, no comparison against alternative architectures, and no adversarial red-team test of the deployed defenses within this paper (the validation against Shapira et al.'s case studies is a mapping exercise, not a re-run of those attacks against the hardened fleet). (5) Four MEDIUM findings remained unremediated (open) at time of writing.

datasets_used: None (this is a production deployment/architecture paper, not a paper using a benchmark dataset); the empirical basis is the authors' own fleet of nine production OpenClaw agents and their own security audit logs
quantitative_results: 4 HIGH-severity findings (H1-H4) discovered and remediated same-day in the March 11, 2026 fleet audit
4 MEDIUM-severity findings (M1-M4) left open at time of writing
6 of 9 fleet VMs fully clean with no findings above LOW
gVisor adds ~2-5ms latency to TCP connection establishment vs. 500-3000ms model inference latency
Coverage of 9 of 11 Shapira et al. case studies directly mitigated, 1 partially mitigated (CS#7), 1 scoped out (CS#6)
90-day deployment/hardening window (Feb 3, 2026 baseline image to Mar 9, 2026 hardened-v2 image), three VM-image hardening generations
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines in the ML-evaluation sense; the paper instead compares its own architecture's security posture across its own successive hardening generations (openclaw-base -> openclaw-hardened -> openclaw-hardened-v2 -> Kubernetes target), and maps its defenses against the eleven attack case studies documented in the cited Shapira et al. red-teaming study (arXiv:2602.20021) as an external reference point, not a re-run experimental baseline
